one_liner: A 58-year-old male with a history of tuberculosis treatment 10 years ago presents with progressive peripheral edema, ascites, and exertional dyspnea.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports 4 months of progressive bilateral leg swelling and increasing abdominal girth. He has gained 15 lbs. He notes dyspnea with exertion (climbing one flight of stairs) and early satiety. He was treated for pulmonary tuberculosis 10 years ago with 6 months of standard therapy and was considered cured. He denies chest pain, orthopnea, or PND. He was initially evaluated for cirrhosis but liver biopsy was unremarkable.
stage2.pmh: Pulmonary tuberculosis (treated 10 years ago), hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus
stage2.surgical_history: Liver biopsy (2 months ago — normal), right inguinal hernia repair
stage2.medications: Metformin 1000 mg BID, lisinopril 20 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily (recently started with minimal improvement)
stage2.family_history: No family history of cardiac disease. Mother had diabetes.
stage2.social_history: Born in India, immigrated to the US 20 years ago. Non-smoker. No alcohol. Retired small business owner. Lives with wife and adult son.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Comfortable at rest, abdominal distension. HEENT: JVP elevated to 16 cm H2O with prominent y descent. Kussmaul sign present (JVP increases with inspiration). Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, early diastolic pericardial knock heard at the left sternal border, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Distended, tense ascites, non-tender hepatomegaly, positive fluid wave. Extremities: 3+ bilateral pitting edema to thighs. Skin: No jaundice, no spider angiomata.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Only mildly elevated despite significant fluid overload — characteristic of constriction (ventricles cannot stretch)
Mildly low, contributing to edema but not low enough to explain the degree of ascites
HIGH protein ascites — characteristic of constriction, NOT cirrhosis
≥1.1, indicating portal hypertension; combined with high protein, suggests cardiac cause

imaging_results.result: Normal LVEF (60%), thickened and echogenic pericardium, septal bounce (exaggerated interventricular septal motion with respiration), exaggerated respiratory variation in mitral inflow (>25%), dilated IVC without collapse, bilateral atrial enlargement, normal wall thickness
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Findings consistent with the suspected diagnosis — septal bounce and respiratory variation are hallmarks

imaging_results.result: Diffusely thickened pericardium (6 mm, normal <2 mm) with extensive pericardial calcification, particularly along the right ventricle. Small bilateral pleural effusions. No pulmonary infiltrates.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Pericardial thickening and calcification strongly support this condition, consistent with prior TB

imaging_results.result: Pericardial thickening (5-6 mm) with septal bounce on real-time cine. Late gadolinium enhancement of the pericardium. No myocardial enhancement.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms pericardial thickening and inflammation; no myocardial fibrosis

stage3.key_tests_rationale: Echo with respiratory variation demonstrates constrictive physiology (septal bounce, >25% mitral inflow variation). CT/MRI identify pericardial thickening and calcification. BNP is characteristically lower than expected. Ascitic fluid analysis showing high protein with high SAAG suggests cardiac cause.

stage5.final_clue: CT reveals extensive pericardial calcification, echocardiography shows septal bounce with exaggerated respiratory variation, and ascitic fluid demonstrates high-protein ascites with SAAG ≥1.1 — all classic for this condition from prior tuberculosis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Constrictive pericarditis mimics right heart failure or cirrhosis with peripheral edema, ascites (often disproportionate to edema), hepatomegaly, and elevated JVP. Key exam findings are Kussmaul sign, pericardial knock (early diastolic sound), and prominent y descent in JVP. Prior TB, cardiac surgery, and radiation are major risk factors.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: The critical distinction is constriction vs. restriction (restrictive cardiomyopathy). Key differentiators: constriction shows septal bounce, pericardial thickening, and ventricular interdependence on echo; restriction shows thickened walls and no pericardial abnormality. Right heart catheterization shows equalization of diastolic pressures and discordance of RV/LV systolic pressures with respiration in constriction (concordance in restriction). BNP is typically lower in constriction than restriction.
debrief.pathophysiology: After pericardial inflammation (TB, surgery, radiation, viral), the pericardium becomes fibrosed and calcified, creating a rigid, non-compliant shell around the heart. This limits diastolic filling: early diastole is normal (before the ventricle hits the pericardial constraint), then filling abruptly stops — creating the 'square root sign' on pressure tracings. The rigid pericardium also causes ventricular interdependence: inspiratory increases in RV filling push the septum leftward, reducing LV filling.
debrief.treatment_overview: Definitive treatment is complete pericardiectomy (surgical stripping of the pericardium). Medical therapy with diuretics provides limited symptomatic relief. Surgical mortality is 6-12%, and outcomes are better when performed before end-stage constriction with hepatic cirrhosis or cachexia. Early referral is important. Anti-tuberculous therapy is not needed if TB was previously treated, but empiric treatment may be considered in new cases.
high_yield_pearls: Constrictive pericarditis should be suspected in any patient with 'right-sided heart failure' and preserved LVEF, especially with ascites disproportionate to peripheral edema.
Kussmaul sign (JVP rises with inspiration) is a hallmark of constriction — in normal physiology, JVP falls with inspiration.
The pericardial knock is an early diastolic sound that corresponds to the abrupt cessation of ventricular filling and can be confused with an S3.
High-protein ascites (>2.5 g/dL) with SAAG ≥1.1 is characteristic of cardiac ascites — differentiating it from cirrhotic ascites (low protein).
TB is the leading cause of constrictive pericarditis worldwide; in developed countries, idiopathic/viral and post-cardiac surgery are most common.
sources: Welch & Oh, Constrictive Pericarditis, Cardiology Clinics 2017
Talreja et al., Constrictive Pericarditis in the Modern Era, JACC 2008
